mapping.a_element: strong correlations in ultracold gases (e.g., Mott-Hubbard transition, BCS-BEC crossover)
mapping.b_element: effective nonlocal interactions leading to roton-maxon excitation spectrum and supersolid formation

mapping.a_element: optical lattices or Feshbach resonances as mechanisms to tune interactions
mapping.b_element: Rydberg dressing to induce effective nonlocal interactions

mapping.a_element: phase transitions driven by strong interactions (e.g., Mott insulator to superfluid)
mapping.b_element: transition to a supersolid state driven by roton instability

mapping.a_element: lowest Landau level physics in rotating gases (quasi-2D systems)
mapping.b_element: three-dimensional condensates with roton-maxon spectrum (momentum-space attraction)

brief_justification: While both papers study phase transitions and excitation spectra in ultracold atomic systems, Paper A focuses on strong local correlations (e.g., Mott-Hubbard physics) and lattice effects, whereas Paper B relies on nonlocal, momentum-space interactions leading to roton instabilities. The underlying mechanisms (local Hubbard physics vs. nonlocal Rydberg-induced interactions) are distinct, despite superficial similarities in studying quantum phases.
